title: From Scientist-Founders to Market-Builders: Team Composition in Deep Tech Startups
text: This thesis examines how the balance between scientific expertise and commercial capabilities in founding teams affects the growth trajectories of deep tech startups. It investigates role complementarity, hiring timing, and leadership transitions from research phase to market expansion. The analysis focuses on how team design influences fundraising capacity, go-to-market execution, and long-term venture survival.

title: Innovation Ecosystems and Deep Tech Scale-Up Outcomes
text: This thesis studies how ecosystem factors such as incubators, university networks, corporate partnerships, and public funding programs shape deep tech venture performance. It evaluates whether ecosystem embeddedness improves access to talent, pilot customers, and strategic resources during commercialization. The work compares ecosystem configurations to identify which combinations are most associated with successful scale-up.

title: Regulation as Enabler or Constraint: AI Policy and Deep Tech Venture Trajectories
text: This thesis explores how regulatory environments influence product development, market entry speed, and financing in AI-driven deep tech startups. It compares regulatory burden, compliance pathways, and policy support instruments across different sectors and jurisdictions. The goal is to identify when regulation reduces uncertainty and trust versus when it slows experimentation and growth.

title: Monetization Pathways in Deep Tech: Product, Platform, and Licensing Strategies
text: This thesis analyzes how deep tech startups choose and evolve monetization strategies under long R&D cycles and high technological uncertainty. It compares product-based models, technology licensing, and platform approaches, with attention to capital requirements and time-to-revenue. The research identifies which monetization pathways best align with specific technology maturity levels and market structures.

title: A Success Assessment Framework for French Deep Tech Startups
text: This thesis develops an empirical framework to assess the likelihood of success for French deep tech ventures using startup-level data and qualitative case evidence. It integrates team attributes, ecosystem participation, financing patterns, and regulatory exposure into a multidimensional evaluation model. The framework is designed to support decision-making for entrepreneurs, investors, and policymakers.
